findings.title: Potential sensitive environment value exposure via console output
findings.description: The embedded loader script prints all loaded environment variables to stdout, only partially masking values whose names contain specific substrings (SECRET, PASSWORD, TOKEN, KEY). Sensitive values with non-matching names (for example DATABASE_URL, SENTRY_DSN, PRIVATE_CERT, CLIENT_SECRET_ID variants not matched, or custom secret names) may be displayed in full, causing unintended disclosure in agent output or logs.
findings.remediation: Do not print raw environment variable values by default. Replace value output with key names only, explicit allowlisting of safe variables, or stronger redaction rules for all values unless the user explicitly requests disclosure.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The code explicitly prints all loaded environment variables and only masks names containing SECRET, PASSWORD, TOKEN, or KEY. Sensitive values under other names could be exposed exactly as described.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy to trigger whenever a user runs the loader and stdout is visible in chat, logs, or terminal history.
metadata.meta_impact: Accidental disclosure of secrets or connection strings such as DATABASE_URL, DSNs, certificates, or custom credential names.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill appears generally benign and focused on local .env parsing and validation. No direct prompt injection, external data exfiltration, command injection, or tool restriction violations were identified. The main concern is low-severity exposure risk from printing environment variable values with incomplete masking, plus missing optional manifest metadata.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern missing manifest metadata/documentation rather than exploitable security behavior.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add explicit manifest fields for license, compatibility, and allowed-tools to improve transparency and policy review.

meta_recommendations.title: Disable value printing or use a safe allowlist
meta_recommendations.fix: Replace the loop that prints every loaded variable with either: (a) a summary-only message like `print(f"Loaded {len(env)} variables")`, or (b) an allowlist such as `SAFE_KEYS = {"PORT", "DEBUG", "NODE_ENV"}` and print only those keys. Do not rely on substring-based masking for secret detection.

meta_recommendations.title: If output is required, strengthen secret redaction
meta_recommendations.fix: Redact by default and only reveal values for explicitly non-sensitive variables. For example: `value = "****" if key not in SAFE_KEYS else env[key]`. Also treat names like `DSN`, `CERT`, `CRED`, `DATABASE_URL`, and `PRIVATE` as sensitive if an allowlist is not feasible.

meta_recommendations.title: Add manifest metadata
meta_recommendations.fix: Add YAML frontmatter fields such as `license: MIT`, `compatibility: [Claude.ai, Claude Code]`, and `allowed-tools: [Read]` if appropriate for the skill's intended behavior.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: This skill is largely benign; the only substantive issue is possible disclosure of sensitive environment values to stdout due to incomplete masking logic.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Stop printing raw environment variable values by default, or switch to an explicit allowlist of non-sensitive keys.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The skill implements local .env parsing and validation with no network, execution, or exfiltration behavior. The main risk is accidental exposure in logs/output, not malicious intent.
